coredata.dc:description:
The tumor microenvironment (TME) exhibits characteristics such as hypoxia, weak acidity, and enrichment of glutathione and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which greatly limits the effectiveness of tumor magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and photodynamic therapy (PDT). Carbon dots (CDs) nanozymes are excellent candidate materials with both diagnostic and therapeutic potential. However, CDs nanozymes with both ultra-high relaxation rate and good therapeutic effect are still to be developed. Herein, novel carbon dots (MPC-CDs) were synthesized from polyethyleneimine (PEI), the photosensitizer hexahydroporphyrin (Ce6) and manganese. The Ce6 enabled the MPC-CDs to exhibit excellent PDT therapeutic ability, with a singlet oxygen yield as high as 1.52. The doping of the metal manganese gave the complexes CAT-like activity, and the singlet oxygen rate was further increased in the presence of H2O2, up to 1.97. In addition, manganese endowed the CDs with better MRI capabilities, and the r
1 and r
2 relaxation rates were significantly improved by 7.8-fold and 4.6-fold under acidic and H2O2 conditions. The in vitro and in vivo results showed that MPC-CDs could achieve TME-responsive MR imaging and synergistic anti-tumor effects, providing an effective strategy to further enhance the effectiveness of tumor diagnosis and treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The tumor microenvironment (TME) exhibits characteristics such as hypoxia, weak acidity, and enrichment of glutathione and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which greatly limits the effectiveness of tumor magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and photodynamic therapy (PDT). Carbon dots (CDs) nanozymes are excellent candidate materials with both diagnostic and therapeutic potential. However, CDs nanozymes with both ultra-high relaxation rate and good therapeutic effect are still to be developed. Herein, novel carbon dots (MPC-CDs) were synthesized from polyethyleneimine (PEI), the photosensitizer hexahydroporphyrin (Ce6) and manganese. The Ce6 enabled the MPC-CDs to exhibit excellent PDT therapeutic ability, with a singlet oxygen yield as high as 1.52. The doping of the metal manganese gave the complexes CAT-like activity, and the singlet oxygen rate was further increased in the presence of H2O2, up to 1.97. In addition, manganese endowed the CDs with better MRI capabilities, and the r
1 and r
2 relaxation rates were significantly improved by 7.8-fold and 4.6-fold under acidic and H2O2 conditions. The in vitro and in vivo results showed that MPC-CDs could achieve TME-responsive MR imaging and synergistic anti-tumor effects, providing an effective strategy to further enhance the effectiveness of tumor diagnosis and treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1. Schematic diagram of MR-guided enhancement of PDT by CAT-like activity of MPC-CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) technology has become an indispensable tool in the diagnosis and treatment of tumors, which has a wide range of applications in clinical for several advantages, such as high soft-tissue resolution, high contrast, no radiation damage and so on [1]. However, the disordered and rapid growth of tumors leads to the tumor microenvironment (TME) exhibiting the features of hypoxia, weak acidity, glutathione (GSH), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) enrichment [2–4], which can significantly deteriorate the contrast of the tumor site, affecting the effectiveness of tumor imaging. In addition, conventional contrast agents (CAs) suffer from defects such as nephrotoxicity, adverse reactions, inefficiency, etc [5,6]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop more efficient tumor imaging systems to cope with the complex TME and thus enhance tumor localization and therapeutic efficacy.

While performing enzyme-like activities, nanozymes also possess the properties of nanomaterials, which exhibit tremendous potential in biomedical research. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) nanozymes are considered as a new type of non-metallic carbon nanomaterials due to their small particle size, abundant surface groups [7,8], easy modification, excellent biocompatibility, and tunable optical properties, etc [9]. A series of explorations and attempts have been carried out for the applications of CDs in tumor imaging. For example, Xu [10] et al. coupled the anti-human monoclonal antibody epitope protein (HE4) with Mn-CDs, which showed high sensitivity and specificity for ovarian cancer, providing selective imaging not only by the fluorescence but also by MRI in vitro and in vivo. Importantly, the Mn-CDs complexes exhibited low toxicity and could be metabolically cleared via the renal pathway. However, the current CDs nanozymes used for MRI imaging displayed low relaxation rates and do not have the ability to enhance the TME response, or the enhancement is not very high [11]. There is an urgent need to develop CDs nanozymes with enhanced TME responsiveness to satisfy the practical clinical needs.

"Theranostics", that combines diagnostics and therapy, has become one of the most important topics in various nanomaterials [12]. The diagnostic function provides information about the location/type/size of a tumor within the organism, while the therapeutic function focuses on the effective delivery and controlled release of drugs/agents to the lesion site. Up to now, CDs nanozymes have been widely used in tumor therapy, including chemical dynamic therapy (CDT), photodynamic therapy (PDT), chemical dynamic therapy (CDT), photothermal therapy (PTT), glutathione depletion, oxygen (O2) supply, metabolic inhibition therapy, and so on [13–16]. Among them, PDT is a therapeutic method that induces damage or even death of tumor cells via utilizing photosensitizers to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) from O2 under light stimulation and thus increase the cytotoxicity of the target cells. Compared with traditional therapies, PDT possesses the advantages of fewer side effects, less damage to normal tissues, and lower drug resistance, and can be applied in combination with other therapies [17–19]. However, as an O2-consuming therapy, the practical application of PDT in the hypoxic tumor microenvironments would be significantly restricted. Due to the heterogeneous distribution of blood vessels in tumors, the strategy of delivering O2 to hypoxic regions of tumors is still doubtful, whereas catalase (CAT) activity of nanozymes could catalyze H2O2 within the TME to provide O2 for PDT treatment. Among the photosensitizers used for PDT, Chlorin e6 (Ce6) has a wide range of applications in PDT due to many advantages such as good photosensitivity, high ROS generation rate, and good biocompatibility [20,21]. However, the application of Ce6 in tumor hypoxic microenvironment is limited due to its high dependence on O2 content. Hence, Gao et al. [22] designed liposomes loaded with Ce6 and MnO2 well to utilize H2O2 in TME to alleviate hypoxia limitation, and the cancer therapeutic efficacy was significantly improved compared with pure Ce6. However, the synthesis process of the liposome was relatively complicated. Therefore, how to construct self-O2 producing and efficient photosensitizers in a simple way that does not depend on O2 content is crucial for PDT therapy.

The aim of this study is to synthesize a new CDs nanozyme as Ce6-doped CDs by screening a range of metals and to investigate its role in enhancing tumor imaging and facilitating PDT therapy. It has been found that Ce6-derived CDs with Mn doped, and PEI was used as passivation agent (MPC-CDs) can generate more singlet oxygen (1O2) under 660 nm light and H2O2 conditions ( Scheme 1). According to the XPS results, the MPC-CDs contained Mn3O4 active ingredient. Mn3O4 could react with H2O2 to generate MnO2, H2O and O2, which is the first step of the O2 release process. Immediately thereafter, MnO2 further decomposes H2O2 to generate O2 in an acidic TME, demonstrating excellent CAT-like activity. These multiple O2 release cascade reactions could significantly improve the hypoxic microenvironment within tumor cells, thus providing more sufficient O2 raw materials for PDT treatment. In addition, MPC-CDs could also perform the function of GSH depletion, which is beneficial to amplify the therapeutic effect of ROS. Additionally, the doped Mn imparts MPC-CDs MRI imaging capability, and the overall relaxation rate r
1 and r
2 is increased by 7.8-fold and 4.6-fold under TME conditions. In vivo experiments confirmed that MPC-CDs could act as photosensitizers to generate ROS at the tumor site while enabling MR imaging, leading to effective MRI-imaging guided PDT therapy. This study provides a new strategy for the application of CDs nanozymes in cancer diagnosis and therapy.

Citric acid, MnCl2-4H2O, polyetherimide (PEI), 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP), 1, 3-diphenylisobenzene (DPBF) and methylene blue (MB), dihydroethidium (DHE) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation (Saint Louis, United States of America). Chlorin e6 (Ce6) and 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) were purchased from laddin Biochemical Technology Corporation (Shanghai, China). Tryptophan (L-Ser) was purchased from BBI Life Sciences Corporation (Shanghai, China). p-Benzoquinone (p-BQ) was purchased from Ron Pharm Corporation (Shanghai, China). Reduced GSH content assay kit, 2-(4-amidophenyl)-6-aminoaminomethylamine dihydrochloride (DAPI), and 4 % paraformaldehyde were purchased from Solarbio Science&Technology Corporation (Beijing, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), penicillin, streptomycin, and trypsin were purchased from Gibco Life Technologies Corporation (Rockville, MD, USA). All chemicals used are unpurified and of analytical grade.

Morphological measurements were performed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tecnai12; Philips, Best, The Netherlands) and an AFM microscope (SPM-9700HT, Shimadzu, Japan). Particle size distribution and zeta potential were determined by a Malvern particle analyzer (ES90 Nano; Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by using a polycrystalline X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance; Bruker-AXS, Karlsruhe, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were determined using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250Xi; Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in the 400–4000 cm−1 range were obtained using a micro-infrared spectrometer (670-IR + 610-IR; Varian, Palo Alto, CA, USA). UV–visible-near-infrared absorption spectra were recorded by an enzyme labeling instrument (SPARK 10 M; Tecan, Männedorf, Switzerland). Catalase (CAT)-like activity enzyme activity was measured by a portable dissolved O2 meter (Mettler S4; Mettler Toledo, Switzerland). ESR spectra were determined on an electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (A300–10/12; Bruker, Bremen, Germany). Cellular experiments and in vivo correlative fluorescence staining images were obtained by fluorescence microscopy (SERIES 120; Carl Zeiss) photography. Nuclear magnetic resonance (MR) images were obtained using a nuclear magnetic resonance instrument (SIGNA Architect 3.0 T; General Electric, Barrington, IL, USA).

MPC-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal synthesis method. First, Ce6 (0.06 g, 0.0001 mol) was fully dissolved in 1 ml of DMSO. Then citric acid (1.0507 g, 0.005 mol), PEI (0.6 g, 0.001 mol) and MnCl2-4H2O (0.3958 g, 0.002 mol) were added into 19 ml of deionized water, and finally Ce6 solution was added, and dissolved by thorough stirring. The mixture was closed in a stainless steel autoclave and reacted at a high temperature of 180°C for 10 h. After natural cooling, the impurities were filtered with a 0.2 μm filter and then dialyzed with a 500–1000 Da dialysis bag for 6 h. Finally, MPC-CDs was obtained by lyophilization in a freeze dryer.

The CAT-like activity of MPC-CDs was determined for MPC-CDs (0, 10, 20, 40, 80 μg/ml) in 0.1 M HAc-NaAc (pH 7.0) buffer solution in the presence of H2O2 (0.8 M) at 37°C. In addition, the CAT-like activity of MPC-CDs (40 μg/ml) was determined in 0.1 M HAc-NaAc (pH 7.0) buffer solution in the presence of different concentrations of H2O2 (0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.6 M).

In PDT, photosensitizers absorb light and accompanying electron transfer to produce highly ROS, including s1O2 and O2 •- [23]. To determine the products of PDT, the following experiments were performed: ROS were effectively detected by DCFH-DA and DHE. L-Trp and p-BQ can act as scavengers of 1O2 and O2 •- respectively [24,25]. DCFH-DA and DHE were added under 660 nm laser irradiation, and then their fluorescence intensity was detected, followed by the addition of L-Trp, p-BQ and detection of fluorescence intensity.
ESR spectra were recorded on an electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer after the MPC-CDs were mixed with the 1O2 trapping agent TEMP irradiated by a laser at a wavelength of 660 nm (5 min).

ROS production by MPC-CDs after 660 nm laser irradiation was determined using DCFH-DA as a ROS probe under excitation at 525 nm. In addition, DPBF as a 1O2 trap has a characteristic absorption peak at 410 nm. When 1O2 is existed, the structure is altered and the absorbance value is attenuated. The degree of attenuation is proportional to the amount of 1O2. Therefore, DPBF was used to determine the 1O2 level produced by MPC-CDs (100 μg/ml) after 660 nm laser.

The 1O2 yield of MPC-CDs was measured using DPBF. A 1O2 yield of 0.49 was used as the standard for MB production in ethanol. MB, Ce6, PC-CDs and MPC-CDs were dissolved in ethanol, and their absorption values under irradiation at 660 nm were fixed below 0.1 OD. Then, an equal amount of DPBF was added and laser irradiation (0.3 W/cm2, 660 nm) was performed. The absorption intensity of DPBF at 416 nm was recorded every 2 min. The slopes of the linear fitting curves were then calculated to derive the 1O2 yields of each group. The 1O2 quantum yield of MPC-CDs (φMPC-CDs) was calculated as
(1)
φ M PC − CDs =
K
MPC − CDs
K
MB
× φ MB
where φMPC-CDs and φMB are the 1O2 yields of Mn-CDs and MB in ethanol. kMPC-CDs and kMB are the DPPF decay rates of Mn-CDs and MB under 660 nm irradiation, respectively.

GSH depletion was measured using the Reduced GSH Assay Kit. Reagents II and III were added to GSH solution (50 μg/ml) and MPC-CDs aqueous solution (0, 25, 50, 100, 200 μg/ml) sequentially. The mixed solution was left for 60 min, and the absorbance was measured at 412 nm. The depletion of GSH was then calculated from the standard curve.

Cytotoxicity was assayed in vitro by MTT assay.4T1 cells were cultured using DMEM High Glucose medium at 37°C with 5 % CO2 and inoculated into 96-well plates at 1×104 cells/well. Then they were incubated with different concentrations of Ce6 and MPC-CDs under normoxic (21 % O2) and hypoxic (2 % O2, containing 100 mM H2O2) conditions, respectively, for 24 h. After rinsing the cells with PBS, fresh medium was added and they were exposed to 660 nm (0.3 W/cm2) laser irradiation for 3 min/per well. Finally, the cells were incubated with MTT for 4 h. The absorbance at 490 nm was recorded on an enzyme meter.

Apoptosis was detected in vitro using calcineurin AM and PI staining. 4T1 cells were inoculated in 12-well plates at a density of 5 × 104 cells per well and cultured overnight. An amount of Ce6 and MPC-CDs were added and cultured under normoxic and hypoxic conditions, respectively, for 24 h. After irradiation with a 660 nm (0.3 W/cm2) laser for 5 min, the cells were washed sufficiently with PBS and stained with AM and PI for 30 min in the dark, and then washed with PBS and imaged under the fluorescence microscope.

4T1 cells (5 × 104 cells/well) were inoculated in 12-well plates overnight, then co-incubated with Ce6 and MPC-CDs in normoxia and hypoxia for 24 h, respectively, irradiated by laser at 660 nm (0.3 W/cm2) for 5 min, washed with PBS, and then stained with DHE and DCFH-DA for 20 min, respectively, in a dark environment, washed again with PBS, fixed by adding fixative for 20 min, then washed again and stained with DAPI. Finally, they were imaged under a fluorescence microscope with DHE and DCFH-DA excitation wavelengths of 535 nm (red channel) and 488 nm (green channel), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of relaxation rates of MPC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The T1- and T2-weighted relaxation rates of MPC-CDs were measured using a 3.0 T clinical MR instrument. Different concentrations of MPC-CDs were added to PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, pH = 7.4 + H2O2, pH = 6.5, pH = 6.5 + H2O2, pH = 5.0, pH = 5.0 + H2O2), and T1- and T2-weighted images were obtained using the 3D Ax T1 FSPGR sequence and Cor T2 STIR sequence. The scanning parameters were T1-weighted (3D Ax T1 FSPGR): TR = minimum value, TE = minimum full value, matrix size = 240 × 240, layer thickness = 0.8 mm, thickness = 5 cm; T2-weighted (Cor T2 STIR): TR = 2933 ms, TE = 85 ms, matrix size = 320 × 320, layer thickness = 1.0 mm, thickness = 2 cm. And the longitudinal and transverse relaxation times (T) of MPC-CDs were recorded with Ax MAGiC sequence (TR of 4251 ms, TE of 18.4 ms). Finally, the relaxation rate was calculated according to Eq:
(2)
r =
1
T
×
C
where T (s) is relaxation time and C (mM) is the concentration of CA.

First, 2×106 4T1 cells were injected subcutaneously into the right side of 4-week-old BALB/c mice to establish a hormonal mouse model. All animal experimental operations comply with the rules of the Yangzhou University Laboratory Animal Ethics Committee (No. 2023- YKL03-G035). After approximately 1 week of tumor growth, 25 mg/kg MPC-CDs were injected into the mice through the tail vein. Imaging was performed on a 3 T clinical magnet MRI machine, and T1/T2-weighted images were detected by 3D Ax T1 FSPGR (TR = minimum, TE = minimum full, matrix size = 240 × 240, layer thickness = 0.8 mm, thickness = 5 cm) and Cor T2 STIR (TR = 2933 ms, TE = 85 ms, matrix size = 320 × 320, layer thickness = 1.0 mm, thickness = 2 cm) at MPC-CDs pre-injection and post-injection 0.5 h, 4 h, 6 h, 10 h, 24 h and 36 h. The region of interest (ROI) was then outlined and the signal quantified using GE healthcare software for tumor, kidney and muscle, where the muscle signal was used as the background value. Finally, the change in MRI contrast at different time points after contrast injection, i.e., signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), was calculated according to the following equation:
(3)

Four-week-old 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into 4 groups (n = 5) and injected via the tail vein with saline, MPC-CDs (25 mg/kg), and Ce6 (0.875 mg/kg), respectively. Subsequently, 660 nm (0.3 W/cm2) laser irradiation was applied to the MPC-CDs and Ce6 groups for 10 min, and the laser intensity and duration were kept constant. Treatments were performed every other day for a total of three treatments. Mouse body weight and tumor size were recorded every other day. The mice were executed after 15 days of treatment, and the major organs and tumors were collected for histological evaluation using H&E staining, immunofluorescence staining and immunohistochemical staining.

All animal experiments were approved by the Ethics Committee of Yangzhou University School of Medicine. Healthy 4-week-old BALB/c mice were randomly divided into three groups (n = 5), and PBS and MPC-CDs (20 mg/kg, 40 mg/kg) were injected into the mice through the tail vein, respectively. The body weights of the mice were measured every other day.After 30 days, the mice were euthanized and their blood samples were collected for biochemical assays (leukocytes, erythrocytes, hemoglobin, plasma volume, plasma cholesterol, plasma cholesterol, plasma cholesterol, hematocrit, ghrelin, ghrelin, ghrelin, and urea nitrogen). Meanwhile, the major organs of mice (heart, liver, spleen, lungs and kidneys) were collected for H&E staining and histologic evaluation.

All of the data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis was performed using Origin and GraphPadPrism software version 9, with statistical significance defined as *p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001, and ****p<0.0001.

MPC-CDs were successfully prepared by a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) showed the morphology and structure of MPC-CDs ( Fig. 1A), dispersed with spherical structure. The particle size ranged from 7.99 to 16.16 nm, with an average size of 11.69 nm. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis in Fig. 1B showed that the average size of MPC-CDs was 21.76 nm. In addition, atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging technique provides a way to observe the surface structure with high resolution under physiological conditions [26]. The results showed that the maximum radius of MPC-CDs was about 19.74 nm, and the highest protrusion point was 25.95 nm (Fig. 1C). The differences in the measured particle size among the three methods may be related to the solvent and the environment around the nanoparticles during the testing process. Zeta potentials in Fig. 1D showed the surface potentials of PC-CDs and MPC-CDs were 5.95 ± 0.16 mV and 2.84 ± 0.55 mV, respectively. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) observed that the MPC-CDs shows a weak diffraction peak at 23.69° in Fig. 1E, which are typical of amorphous CDs [27–29]. The chemical compositions and elemental states of the MPC-CDs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which showed typical peaks for C 1 s (285.08 eV, 60.95 %), N 1 s (399.08 eV, 16.48 %), O 1 s (531.08 eV, 17.54 %) and Mn 2p (641.08 eV, 2.35 %), respectively (Fig. 1F). Among them, the main peaks at 284.33, 285.53 and 287.38 eV in the C 1 s spectra (Fig. 1G) represent C-C, C-O and CO, respectively [30], and the main peak signal at 399.58 eV in the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 1H) belongs to N-H [31]. The two peaks at 530.78 and 532.03 eV in the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1I) are attributed to CO and C-OH [32]. Importantly, the XPS spectrum of Mn 2p (Fig. 1J) showed three major peaks at 640.88, 644.88 and 653.13 eV, attributed to Mn3O4, Mn 2p and Mn 2p1/2 [33–35]. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) is an important tool to study the structure and chemical bonding of nanocomposites. The chemical bonding present in Ce6 was shown in Fig. 1K, which displayed a strong telescopic vibrational peak of carboxyl group (-COOH) at 1710 cm−1. The characteristic vibrational fronts of O-H are observed at 3288 cm−1 and 1234 cm−1, and characteristic bands of C-H band are observed at 2960 cm−1 and 1429 cm−1 [36]. Similar vibrational peaks were observed for MPC-CDs, and the presence of small deviations may be due to smaller band shifts occurring during the functionalization process [36]. The results indicated that Ce6 is doped into the CDs. Fig. 1L showed the UV absorption and emission spectra of MPC-CDs, which have a broad absorption band between 300 and 800 nm, and the absorption peak at 348 nm is related to the n-π* leap of CO in the carboxyl group [37]. The maximum emission wavelength of MPC-CDs is 432 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CAT-like activity and PDT-promoting effects of MPC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: PDT has been successfully used in clinical cancer therapy due to its noninvasive features and lower side effects [38]. The effect of PDT lies in the production and accumulation of ROS, which is O2-dependent and influenced by the hypoxic TME [39]. And CAT-like activity can promote the decomposition of H2O2 into O2 and water (H2O) in TME [40]. Thus, the CAT-like activity and PDT-promoting effects of MPC-CDs were determined. Fig. 2A showed that amount and rate of O2 production increased as the concentration of MPC-CDs increasing. Fig. 2B displayed that the production of O2 was positively proportional to the H2O2 concentration, which was related to the Mn3O4 in the MPC-CDs, catalyzing H2O2 to produce MnO2, H2O, and O2. In addition, MnO2 further catalyzes H2O2 to produce O2 [41]. PDT is a series of chemical reactions, including type I (O2 •- producing) and type II (1O2 producing) reactions, and type I and type II reactions can occur simultaneously, which depend on the photosensitizer, the substrate, the O2 concentration, and the binding affinity of the sensitizer to the substrate [42]. The products of PDT were determined using tryptophan (L-Trp) and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) as scavengers of 1O2 and O2 •-, respectively. And Fig. 2C showed that the PDT products were predominantly 1O2 and very small amounts of O2 •-. After determining the type of the products, the photodynamic activity of MPC-CDs was measured using 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP, 1O2 trapping agent). The ESR results (Fig. 2D) showed that MPC-CDs appeared as typical 1O2 triple signal fronts with an area ratio of 1:1:1 under 660 nm laser irradiation. Notably, the area of the signal front of the MPC-CDs mixture with the addition of H2O2 was larger. To further verify the photodynamic activity of MPC-CDs, dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and 1, 3-diphenylisobenzene (DPBF) were used as 1O2 trapping agents. The results (Fig. 2E and 2F) showed that the 1O2 production capacity of Ce6 was high under laser irradiation. MPC-CDs were able to produce 1O2 under 660 nm laser irradiation with better photodynamic activity. Compared with MPC-CDs alone, the mixture of H2O2 and MPC-CDs had better photodynamic activity under the laser irradiation. The 1O2 yields of MPC-CDs and MPC-CDs + H2O2 were 1.52 and 1.97, respectively (Fig. 2G). In contrast, the low 1O2 yield of MPC-CDs and MPC-CDs + H2O2 compared to Ce6 (1O2 rate of 2.15) may be related to the lower content of the components that contain the producible 1O2 under the same mass conditions, or to the fact that part of the Ce6 structure is damaged during the carbonization process. The above results suggested that MPC-CDs were photodynamically active in laser irradiation and were able to produce 1O2, while the 1O2 yield was higher after H2O2 addition, which was closely related to the CAT-like activity of MPC-CDs, promoting its PDT effect. In addition, GSH over-expressed in TME has a strong antioxidant capacity and increases resistance to PDT injury [43]. Therefore, GSH depletion is of great significance for improving the effect of PDT [44]. Fig. 2G and 2I showed that as the concentration of MPC-CDs increased, GSH was depleted more significantly. In summary, MPC-CDs have CAT-like activity, which can produce O2 and promote PDT effects. Moreover, the depletion of GSH can accelerate the accumulation of ROS, which provides a strong basis for achieving tumor therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro PDT-enhancing effects of MPC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The viability of 4T1 cells in the presence of Ce6 and MPC-CDs was determined by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 3A and 3B, without laser irradiation (660 nm, 0.3 W/cm2, 3 min), Ce6 and MPC-CDs had no obvious killing effect on 4T1 cells, and the survival rate of 4T1 cells at each concentration was more than 75 %. In contrast, both Ce6 and MPC-CDs showed concentration-dependent toxicity to 4T1 cells after laser irradiation. It was worth noting that under hypoxic conditions (containing 100 mM H2O2), the cytotoxicity of Ce6 was significantly reduced after laser irradiation, while MPC-CDs still showed the best cytotoxicity after laser irradiation, which suggested that Ce6 fails to utilize H2O2 efficiently under hypoxic conditions, whereas MPC-CDs have good CAT-like activity and can catalyze H2O2 well to produce O2, alleviate hypoxia limitation and enhance the PDT effect. In addition, co-staining of calcein AM (staining live cells with green fluorescence) and PI (staining dead cells with red fluorescence) was used to further validate the in vitro PDT enhancement effect of MPC-CDs. Strong red fluorescence could be observed in the laser-exposed regions of both Ce6 and MPC-CDs under normal O2. In contrast, the red fluorescence in the light-exposed region without light was not significantly different from that of the control group, suggesting that Ce6 and MPC-CDs have a strong tumor cell killing effect under laser irradiation and can well exert the PDT effect (Fig. 3C and Fig. S1A). However, under hypoxic conditions, the red fluorescence of Ce6 was significantly reduced after laser irradiation compared with that under normoxic conditions, and the difference with the control group was not significant. However, significantly strong red fluorescence was still observed in the laser-irradiated area of the MPC-CDs (Fig. 3F and Fig. S1B). The above Calcein/PI results were consistent with the MTT results, indicating that MPC-CDs were able to alleviate the hypoxia limitation and produce strong tumor cell killing effects. In addition, 1O2 production during PDT was clarified with the ROS probes DCFH-DA and DHE. Fig. 3D and 3G showed that under normoxia, Ce6 and MPC-CDs were able to produce large amounts of 1O2 after 660 nm laser irradiation. Under hypoxic conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the Ce6 laser-irradiated group decreased dramatically, while the MPC-CDs laser-irradiated group still had strong fluorescence intensity. The DCFH-DA staining results (Fig. 3E and 3H) were consistent with DHE staining results. The great difference between the two groups was that PDT depended on O2 supply, while MPC-CDs were able to utilize H2O2 in TME to provide O2. The above results showed that the PDT effect was obviously limited by hypoxia, while MPC-CDs were able to utilize endogenous H2O2 from TME for effective self-supplied O2. Moreover, MPC-CDs were able to effectively utilize self-supplied O2 to generate 1O2 under the action of laser to realize the PDT-enhancing effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TME responsive MR imaging of MPC-CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
TME was simulated with different pH and addition of H2O2, and the MRI capability of MPC-CDs was evaluated using a 3.0 T GE MRI system. T1- and T2-weighted imaging of MPC-CDs (concentrations of Mn were 0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 mM) under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 4, the T1- and T2-weighted image changes were in a concentration dependent manner. Increasing concentration of Mn resulted in brighter T1-weighted images and darker T2-weighted images. The brightness and darkness changes in T1- and T2-weighted images were more significant in acidic (pH = 6.5, 5.0) conditions compared to neutral (pH = 7.4) conditions (Fig. 4A, 4B, and 4C), which may be related to pH responsiveness. XPS results showed that MPC-CDs contain Mn3O4, which can react with H+ to produce Mn2+ (Mn3O4 + 4 H+ → 2Mn2+ + MnO2 + 2 H2O) [45,46]. Notably, the imaging ability of the MPC-CDs mixture with the addition of H2O2 was further improved (Fig. 4D, 4E, and 4F), which correlates with the H2O2 responsiveness. The reaction of MnO2 with H2O2 is also capable of generating Mn2+ (MnO2 + 2 H+ + H2O2 → Mn2+ + O2 + 2 H2O) [47]. In addition, the longitudinal and transverse relaxation rates r
1 and r
2 of MPC-CDs were calculated based on the concentration of Mn and the longitudinal and transverse relaxation time T1 and T2 (Fig. 4G and 4H). The results showed that the r
1 and r
2 relaxation rates of MPC-CDs were significantly increased by 7.8-fold and 4.6-fold under acidic and H2O2 conditions, respectively, compared with neutral conditions. Moreover, MRI ability of MPC-CDs was superior to the clinically used T1 MRI CA, Gd-DTPA (r1 = ≈3–5 mM−1 s−1). The above results indicated that MPC-CDs are TME-responsive (acidic and H2O2-responsive) and are capable of generating Mn2+ through a series of reactions. Importantly, five unpaired 3d electrons are present in Mn2+, and the more unpaired 3d electrons, the better the T1- and T2-weighted MRI ability [48,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: MR imaging of MPC-CDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: MPC-CDs were injected into 4T1 tumor-bearing mice through the tail vein, and T1- and T2-weighted images were acquired at different times before and after the injection. In order to more intuitively evaluate the T1- and T2-weighted imaging effects of MPC-CDs in vivo, the coronal and cross-sectional MR images of mice and the corresponding pseudo-color maps were listed ( Fig. 5A). As shown in Fig. 5A, in the T1-weighted image, the brightness of the tumor site gradually increased within 6 h after injection. The brightness of the tumor site peaked at about 6 h after injection and then gradually decreased with time. The brightness of the tumor site in the T2-weighted images was opposite to that in the T1-weighted images. The brightness of the tumor site in T2-weighting was darkest at about 6 h post-injection, and gradually brightened thereafter. In addition, the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at the tumor site was quantified for a more accurate understanding of in vivo MR imaging of MPC-CDs (Fig. 5B and 5C). From the quantitative plot of ΔSNR, it can be seen that both T1- and T2-weighted ΔSNR of the tumor site began to change after MPC-CDs injection, with the greatest change at 6 h post-injection, followed by a weakening of the change. At 6 h after MPC-CDs injection, the T1-weighted signal intensity of the tumor site increased to 156 %, and the T2-weighted signal intensity decreased to 67 % (Fig. 5B and 5C). Signal changes in MRI of tumor sites may be associated with the in vivo metabolism of MPC-CDs. CDs are mainly excreted through urine [50]. Thus, the signals at the kidney site of T1- and T2-weighted images were quantified. The quantitative ΔSNR map of the renal region showed that the signals in the renal region also changed significantly, with the greatest difference in signals at 6 h, after which the difference gradually decreased (Fig. 5D and 5E) suggesting that the signal attenuation after 6 h after tumor site injection is closely related to kidney metabolism. Jiang [51] et al. successfully synthesized gadolinium-doped MR imaging CDs, and the tumor site signal could reach the peak 4 h after injection. In contrast, MPC-CDs can stay in the body for more than 6 h, which provides a good basis for tumor therapy. The above results indicated that MPC-CDs can accumulate well in the tumor site through permeability and retention (EPR) effect, and perform a good MR signal contrast in the tumor site. Notably, they can be metabolized by kidney, which is expected to be a safe and effective passive tumor-targeting CAs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of PDT effects by MPC-CDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: MR imaging verified that MPC-CDs could effectively accumulate at the tumor site. Moreover, in vitro experiments verified that MPC-CDs had CAT-like activity and high 1O2 yield under the laser irradiation. To further verify the therapeutic effect of MPC-CDs in PDT, a subcutaneous 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse models were established. The tumor-bearing mice were divided into four groups: control, MPC-CDs, Ce6 + Laser (660 nm), and MPC-CDs + Laser. Fig. 6A shows images of the tumor sites in each group of mice after receiving the corresponding treatment. As shown in Fig. 6A, the tumors in the control and MPC-CDs groups were larger and even ruptured. The tumors of the Ce6 + Laser group were reduced compared with the control group, but the effect was not very satisfactory. The tumors in the MPC-CDs + Laser group were significantly reduced and almost invisible. In order to observe the tumor size of each group more intuitively, the tumors were dissected (Fig. 6B) and their weights were quantitatively analyzed. As observed in the Fig. 6C, there was little difference in the size and weight of tumors between the MPC-CDs group and the control group. The size and weight of tumor in the Ce6 + Laser group were reduced but still larger compared to those in the MPC-CDs + Laser group. The size and weight of tumor in the MPC-CDs + Laser group were significantly reduced, and some were even eliminated. In addition, the volume of tumor change curves in each group throughout the treatment (Fig. 6D) more comprehensively verified the PDT therapeutic effect of MPC-CDs. In the control group and the MPC-CDs group, the volume of tumor has been increasing rapidly. The tumor growth in the Ce6 + Laser group has been slowed down. In the MPC-CDs + Laser group, unlike the other three groups, the volume of tumor has been reduced after the treatment, and the growth has been obviously inhibited. The above results showed that MPC-CDs could well inhibit tumor growth and play the role of PDT under the effect of laser irradiation. The difference in tumor changes between the MPC-CDs + Laser group and the Ce6 + Laser group was mainly attributed to the ability of MPC-CDs to utilize H2O2 in the TME, which could provide O2 for the PDT treatment. Moreover, MPC-CDs also have the ability to scavenge GSH, which contributes to the accumulation of ROS and achieves better tumor therapeutic effects. In addition, H&E staining, Ki67 immunohistochemical staining and tunel staining were performed on the tumors in each group (Fig. 6E). H&E staining showed that the apoptotic area in the MPC-CDs + Laser group and the Ce6 + Laser group was larger than that in the control group and the MPC-CDs group, which was more obvious in the MPC-CDs + Laser group. The cell proliferation antigen Ki67 can indicate the proliferation of cells. The staining results showed that the proliferation ability of MPC-CDs + Laser group was significantly decreased compared with the other three groups. Tunel staining was used as a common method to detect apoptosis staining. From the results of tunel staining, it can be seen that the number of apoptotic cells in the MPC-CDs + Laser group was extremely high. The above staining results indicated that MPC-CDs were able to cause apoptosis of tumor cells and inhibit their proliferation ability under laser irradiation. In addition, there was no significant difference in body weight (Fig. 6F) and H&E staining of major organs (Fig. S2) in each group of mice, indicating that MPC-CDs did not cause damage to the mouse organism. The results of the in vivo experiments showed that MPC-CDs were able to generate ROS under the action of laser to realize PDT therapy. Importantly, by utilizing H2O2 in TME, the hypoxic limitation of PDT was improved and the effect of PDT treatment was enhanced. Compared with photodynamic therapy alone [52–54], MPC-CDs can better inhibit tumor cell proliferation and mediate apoptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biological safety of MPC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In vivo long-term safety is a prerequisite for the biological application of MPC- CDs. For this reason, the body weight, blood and biochemical indices of mice were examined after different concentrations of MPC-CDs injection via the tail vein for safety assessment. As shown in Fig. S3, the trend of body weight change was similar in all groups without significant difference. The examination of blood routine and blood biochemical indexes suggested the long-term toxicity of MPC-CDs in vivo. As shown in Fig. 7A, there were no significant differences in markers of liver and renal function, including blood urea nitrogen, alkaline phosphatase, glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase, and alanine aminotransferase, between the groups compared with the control group. The blood biochemical indices, i.e., erythrocytes, mean platelet volume, mean erythrocyte volume, mean erythrocyte hemoglobin concentration, erythrocyte pressurized volume, leukocytes, mean erythrocyte hemoglobin, and hemoglobin were also in the normal range in each group, which indicates that the MPC-CDs have a negligible hematotoxicity in vivo. In addition, H&E staining was performed on major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys, and intestines) in each group to detect the histocompatibility of MPC-CDs. Fig. 7B showed that similar to the control group, there was no significant inflammation, tissue damage and lesions in the MPC-CDs-injected group. The above results indicated that MPC-CDs are biocompatible and safe, laying the foundation for in vivo imaging and treatment of tumors.

In summary, a new strategy for the integration of tumor diagnosis and treatment was successfully developed with a new type of carbon-dotted nanozymes (MPC-CDs). MPC-CDs display TME (acidic and H2O2) responsive MR imaging ability. Under neutral conditions, the longitudinal and transverse relaxation rates (r
1 and r
2 ) of MPC-CDs were 1.073 mM−1 s−1 and 17.67 mM−1 s−1, respectively. Under acidic conditions, the active ingredient of MPC-CDs (Mn3O4) could generate Mn2+. The r
1 and r
2 of MPC-CDs increased to 6.237 mM−1 s−1 and 80.54 mM−1 s−1·H2O2 also caused Mn2+ to be generated, and r
1 and r
2 reached 8.322 mM−1 s−1 and 80.47 mM−1 s−1, respectively, indicating that MPC-CDs are able to achieve significant MR contrast at the tumor site with participation of TME, which provides reliable information for tumor localization mention and facilitates tumor detection. In addition, MPC-CDs can be used as photosensitizers to generate 1O2 under the action of laser, with a yield of 1.52. It is worth mentioning that MPC-CDs have high CAT-like activity, which can effectively utilize H2O2 in TME to generate O2. Under laser light irradiation, MPC-CDs effectively alleviate the limitation of hypoxia in PDT, and the yield of 1O2 was significantly enhanced, with a yield of 1.97. In addition, MPC-CDs could deplete GSH in TME and promote the effective accumulation of 1O2, thus enhancing the tumor therapeutic ability of MPC-CDs. The H2O2 responsiveness and GSH depletion of MPC-CDs enhanced their PDT effect. In addition, in vivo MR imaging and therapeutic experiments verified that MPC-CDs were able to effectively accumulate at tumor sites through passive targeting and exert enhanced PDT efficacy to achieve MR-guided tumor inhibition. Therefore, MPC-CDs can effectively utilize the conditions of TME to achieve satisfactory MR imaging effect and PDT treatment effect, and realize effective inhibition of tumor growth along with MR imaging. In summary, MPC-CDs can be used as a novel multifunctional carbon dotted nanozymes in tumor imaging and synergistic therapy.
